metadata.summary: Every task must fit within 100k token context budget. Fixed overhead plus variable codebase reading. Tasks exceeding 80k must be split before any TASK file is written.

content.title: Implementation Plan: 100k Token Rule
content.summary: Every task assigned to an agent must fit within a 100k token context budget. The budget has fixed overhead (agent prompt ~8k, project context ~12k, task file ~3k, buffer ~15k) plus variable codebase reading. Tasks exceeding 80k tokens must be split before any TASK file is written. Three splitting strategies: by component, by layer, by dependency wave.

why.p: Agents assigned tasks exceeding their effective context window produce incomplete output, higher hallucination rates, and longer execution times — and the executor does not abort mid-task. Budget enforcement is entirely a planning-phase responsibility. Wave-based task creation (create Wave 1, execute, then create Wave 2 using patterns learned) further reduces token waste from early task files that become stale when Wave 1 reveals wrong assumptions.

list.item: Before assigning any task to faion-sdd-executor-agent — validate context budget fits.
When decomposing a large design into tasks and unsure whether to split or combine.
Auditing an existing implementation plan where tasks seem to exceed context window.
Estimating total project token cost before committing to an implementation approach.
Micro-tasks under 5k tokens — budget calculation overhead is not worth it.
Reading-only research passes — context budget still applies but failure mode is less severe.
Attempting to skip the rule for Claude 200k context — focus degrades at high utilization even within window limits.

section.name: budget-breakdown
section.p: The fixed and variable components of the 100k token budget per task, three worked estimation examples, and complexity level thresholds.

rule.statement: Every token budget estimate must itemize all components: agent prompt (~8k), project context (~12k), task file (~3k), design docs (relevant AD-X sections), codebase reading (list every file with size estimate), buffer (~15k). Sum must be under 100k.
rule.rationale: Unitemized estimates are consistently optimistic by 30-50%. Each component class has different variability. Itemization makes the overrun source diagnosable.

section.p: Fixed overhead per task: agent prompt 8k + project context 12k + task file 3k + buffer 15k = 38k tokens minimum.
Variable cost: codebase reading = sum of tokens in all files the agent will read. One token ≈ 4 bytes (use wc -c as proxy when ttok unavailable).

rule.statement: Include generated files (ORM clients, GraphQL schemas, migration snapshots) explicitly in the variable cost. They are 5-10x larger than hand-written equivalents.
rule.rationale: Agents consistently omit generated file sizes from estimates. A Prisma client or full OpenAPI schema can consume 20-40k tokens alone.

section.p: Complexity thresholds: understanding the three baseline complexity levels helps in categorizing and estimating task scope accurately.

list.item: Simple: total context under 30k tokens. Single file change with clear pattern and minimal dependencies. Examples: database migration, utility function addition, configuration change, simple bug fix.

Normal: 30-60k tokens. Multiple files involved, some integration between components, standard architectural patterns. Examples: API endpoint implementation, service class addition, middleware integration, straightforward feature addition.

Complex: 60-100k tokens. Many files with deep integration, edge case handling, cross-module dependencies. Examples: E2E test suite creation, complex business logic spanning multiple layers, refactoring affecting many files.

rule.statement: Flag tasks with complex tasks that require debugging existing code with a 30k exploration buffer on top of the standard formula. Debugging reads are non-deterministic.
rule.rationale: Standard formula assumes known files to read. Debugging passes read many files iteratively and unpredictably; the standard 15k buffer is insufficient.

example.title: Single file change estimate (~15k)
example.code: Breakdown: - Agent prompt: 5k - Project context: 8k - Task file: 2k - File to modify (500 lines): ~4k - Buffer: 15k Total: ~34k tokens (simple complexity)

example.title: Multi-file change (3-5 files) estimate (~45k)
example.code: Breakdown: - Agent prompt: 5k - Project context: 10k - Task file: 3k - Files to modify: 15k (5 files × 3k avg) - Dependencies: 12k (4 files × 3k avg) - Buffer: 15k Total: ~60k tokens (normal complexity)

example.title: Complex refactoring (10+ files) estimate (~85k)
example.code: Breakdown: - Agent prompt: 8k - Project context: 15k - Task file: 5k - Files to modify: 35k (10 files × 3.5k avg) - Dependencies: 20k (6 files × 3.3k avg) - Buffer: 15k Total: ~98k tokens (complex complexity - borderline, consider splitting)

section.name: splitting-strategies
section.p: When to split tasks, three splitting strategies (by component, by layer, by dependency wave), WBS principles, and the wave-based task creation pattern.

rule.statement: Split a task if: context estimate exceeds 80k tokens, task touches more than 10 files, task requires deep research across many modules, task has multiple independent sub-goals. Calculate budget BEFORE writing the TASK file.
rule.rationale: Tasks over 80k tokens are at risk of context overflow. Estimates that reveal a 120k task should trigger a split before any TASK file is created — not after.

rule.statement: Apply three WBS rules: 100% Rule (all work is accounted for, no orphaned work), Mutually Exclusive (no overlap between tasks), Completeness (each task has clear done criteria). Test-writing tasks and CHANGELOG updates must appear explicitly.
rule.rationale: WBS omissions are the most common source of "surprise" work at the end of a feature. Test writing and documentation are consistently omitted when the plan focuses on production code only.

rule.statement: Create detailed TASK_*.md files in waves, not all at once. Create and execute Wave 1 first; then create Wave 2 files using patterns learned from Wave 1 execution reports.
rule.rationale: Task files created before Wave 1 execution are based on design assumptions that may be wrong. Wave 2 files created after Wave 1 reflect actual implementation patterns, reducing rework.

example.title: Splitting by component
example.code:
Before: TASK-001: Implement user authentication (150k tokens)
After: TASK-001: Create user database schema (25k tokens) TASK-002: Implement password hashing utilities (20k tokens) TASK-003: Create registration handler (30k tokens) TASK-004: Create login handler (35k tokens) TASK-005: Add authentication middleware (25k tokens)
example.note: First sub-task has zero dependencies — Wave 1 can start immediately.

example.title: Splitting by layer
example.code:
Before: TASK-001: Build payment system (180k tokens)
After: TASK-001: Payment database models (30k tokens) TASK-002: Payment service layer (40k tokens) TASK-003: Payment API endpoints (35k tokens) TASK-004: Payment UI components (35k tokens) TASK-005: Payment integration tests (40k tokens)

example.title: Splitting by dependency wave
example.code:
Before: TASK-001: Implement notification system (140k tokens)
After: Wave 1 (parallel): TASK-001: Notification data models (25k tokens) TASK-002: Email sender utility (20k tokens)
Wave 2 (depends on Wave 1): TASK-003: Notification service (35k tokens) TASK-004: Notification templates (25k tokens)
Wave 3 (depends on Wave 2): TASK-005: Notification API (35k tokens)

antipattern.description: Agents asked to "create all tasks now" create Wave 2 and Wave 3 stubs with placeholder content.
antipattern.reason: Placeholder task files degrade the quality of Wave 2 and 3 execution. Enforce wave-gated creation in the planning prompt.

section.name: quality-gates
section.p: Quality gate checks for implementation plans using the 100k rule:

list.item: All tasks have token estimate less than 100k tokens.
Task estimates include fixed overhead (8k prompt + 12k context + 3k task file + 15k buffer).
Generated files are accounted for in variable cost (Prisma, OpenAPI, migration snapshots).
Debugging tasks have 30k exploration buffer on top of standard formula.
Wave-based task creation is enforced — Wave 2 files created after Wave 1 execution.
All WBS rules applied: 100% Rule, Mutually Exclusive, Completeness.
Test-writing tasks and CHANGELOG updates appear explicitly in task breakdown.

template.purpose: Bash script: sums token estimates for a task's file list using byte count proxy; flags tasks over 100k.
